Metadata.Case_Number: Originating Application No 1153 of 2024 (Registrar’s Appeal No 13 of 2025)
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Canstone Management Ltd

Facts.Text: Canstone Management Limited is a shareholder of Allestari Development Pte Ltd and holds 1.7% of the shares in Allestari according to the company register.
Li Yi was appointed as a director of Allestari Development Pte Ltd in 2008 and is described as the president and ultimate beneficial owner of Canstone Management Limited.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a minority shareholder can seek declaratory and mandatory relief to imply new terms into a company's constitution regarding director appointments and to compel the removal of specific directors, while an active court injunction in separate proceedings explicitly preserves the current board composition.
Party_Details.Rule: Courts will not grant declaratory or mandatory relief that operates as a collateral attack on an existing court order (such as an interim injunction preserving the status quo of a board of directors). Furthermore, courts will not imply terms into a company's constitution unless there is a genuine gap that makes the implication strictly necessary for business efficacy, and the proposed term reflects the objective intention of the parties at the time of drafting. Declaratory relief requires a live controversy, not a theoretical or hypothetical dispute.
Party_Details.Application: Canstone Management Ltd, holding a 1.7% stake in Allestari Development Pte Ltd, filed an originating application seeking to imply terms into Article 76A of the company's constitution. This article originally granted a specific former shareholder (Allway) the right to appoint three directors. Canstone sought to rewrite this provision to transfer the appointment rights to the current top three majority shareholders and demanded the removal of the three incumbent directors (including Canstone's own ultimate beneficial owner, Li Yi). Crucially, Canstone launched this application fully aware that another court had already issued a strict interim injunction in a separate suit (HC/OC 798/2023) expressly prohibiting any changes to Allestari's board composition pending the resolution of a major shareholder dispute. The legal conflict assesses whether Canstone's application is a legitimate mechanism for corporate governance or a factually unsustainable abuse of process designed to bypass an active court injunction.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed Canstone's appeal and upheld the striking out of the entire originating application. First, the court found the application to be a blatant abuse of process, acting as an impermissible collateral attack on the existing interim injunction that froze the board's composition. If Canstone wished to alter the board, the proper procedural route was to seek leave to intervene in the suit where the injunction was granted, not to launch a parallel action. Second, the court ruled the claim for implied terms was factually and legally unsustainable under the Sembcorp Marine test. There was no 'gap' in the constitution requiring the court's intervention; both the constitution and the Companies Act provided standard mechanisms (ordinary resolutions) for shareholders to remove and appoint directors. Canstone was attempting to use the court to reallocate shareholder rights and rewrite the constitution, rather than giving it business efficacy. Finally, there was no live controversy regarding the original appointing shareholder (Allway), as Allway had ceased to be a member over a decade ago and was not asserting any ongoing appointment rights. Because the claims were unsustainable, hypothetical, and constituted an abuse of process, the action was struck out with costs awarded against Canstone.

Metadata.Case_Number: Originating Application No 1153 of 2024 (Registrar’s Appeal No 13 of 2025)
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Allestari Development Pte. Ltd.

Facts.Text: Allestari Development Pte Ltd is a company with multiple shareholders, including Canstone Management Limited, Clarity China Partners LP, N Century Holding Company Limited, Yunnan Nonferrous 308 Geological Exploration Team, China, Reliable Source Investments Limited, Brillante Resources Ltd and Allway Minerals and Science Technology Co., Ltd at different times.

According to the company register, Clarity China Partners LP holds 56.62% of the shares in Allestari Development Pte Ltd and N Century Holding Company Limited holds 20.28% of the shares, with the relative shareholdings of these two entities being the subject of dispute in HC/OC 798/2023.
Allway Minerals and Science Technology Co., Ltd was the majority shareholder of Allestari Development Pte Ltd until 2011.

On 2008-07-01, Allestari Development Pte Ltd adopted articles including Article 76A by special resolution, providing that Allway Minerals and Science Technology Co., Ltd was entitled to appoint and remove three members of the board of directors, each of Squad 308 and the BVI Holdco was entitled to appoint and remove one member of the board of directors, and there would be one independent member of the board whose nomination required approval by the board including an affirmative vote from the Investor Director.
In 2008, Allway Minerals and Science Technology Co., Ltd appointed three individuals, including Li Yi, as directors of Allestari Development Pte Ltd pursuant to Article 76A.

After Allway Minerals and Science Technology Co., Ltd ceased to be a shareholder of Allestari Development Pte Ltd in 2011, the three directors originally appointed by Allway remained on the board and were not removed by the shareholders.

Allestari Development Pte Ltd has a memorandum and articles of association dated 2006-12-11, referred to as its Constitution, which includes provisions on nomination, appointment and removal of directors such as Articles 76A and 86 to 88.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the court should imply terms into a company's constitution to reallocate specific director appointment rights to current majority shareholders when the original appointing shareholder has exited, or whether existing statutory and constitutional mechanisms for director removal are sufficient.
Party_Details.Rule: A court will not imply terms into a company's constitution to effectively rewrite the document or reallocate shareholder rights. Implication is only permitted if there is a true 'gap' necessary for business efficacy, and the implied term must reflect the objective intention of the parties at the time the constitution was adopted. Where the constitution and the Companies Act already provide standard mechanisms (such as an ordinary resolution) for the shareholders to remove and appoint directors, no gap exists requiring curial intervention.
Party_Details.Application: Allestari Development Pte. Ltd.'s constitution contained Article 76A, which granted a specific shareholder (Allway) the right to appoint three directors. Allway exercised this right in 2008 but subsequently sold its shares and exited the company in 2011. Despite Allway's exit, the shareholders never voted to remove the three incumbent directors, allowing them to remain on the board for over a decade. A minority shareholder (Canstone) recently filed an application seeking to have the court imply a term into Article 76A that would automatically transfer Allway's specific appointment rights to the current top three majority shareholders, and sought a mandatory order to compel the removal of the three incumbent directors. This application was filed despite an active court injunction in another suit that specifically prohibited any changes to Allestari's board composition.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court ruled that Allestari's constitution did not contain a gap requiring the implication of terms. The departure of the shareholder (Allway) named in Article 76A simply meant that the specific right to appoint those three directors lapsed. However, this did not paralyze the company; the remaining shareholders retained their general rights under Articles 86 to 88 of the constitution and Sections 149B and 152(9) of the Companies Act to appoint and remove directors via ordinary resolution. Canstone was attempting to use the court to forcibly reallocate shareholder rights and bypass the democratic mechanism of an ordinary resolution. Because the proposed implied terms were legally and factually unsustainable, and because the application was an abusive collateral attack on an existing injunction freezing the board's composition, the court struck out the application against Allestari and the other defendants.

Metadata.Case_Number: Originating Application No 1153 of 2024 (Registrar’s Appeal No 13 of 2025)
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: N Century Holding Company Limited

Facts.Text: According to the register of Allestari Development Pte Ltd, N Century Holding Company Limited holds 20.28% of the shares in Allestari Development Pte Ltd, with its relative shareholding compared to Clarity China Partners LP being the subject of dispute in HC/OC 798/2023.

On 2022-11-25, N Century Holding Company Limited applied in HC/OC 798/2023 for an interim injunction restraining Clarity China Partners LP and Allestari Development Pte Ltd from altering the composition of Allestari's board, including by removal or substitution of any director.

N Century Holding Company Limited's application for the interim injunction in HC/OC 798/2023 was made in response to an attempt by Clarity China Partners LP to remove three directors from the board of Allestari Development Pte Ltd, namely Liu Xun, Guo Jian and Li Yi.

N Century Holding Company Limited applied to strike out Originating Application No 1153 of 2024 in its entirety under Rules of Court 2021 Order 9 Rule 16(1), contending that the claim was time-barred, factually and legally unsustainable, and an abuse of process.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether an originating application seeking to mandate changes to a company's board composition should be struck out as an abuse of process when it constitutes a collateral attack on a pre-existing interim injunction obtained by a substantial shareholder.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Order 9 Rule 16(1) of the Rules of Court 2021, the court may strike out a pleading that is legally or factually unsustainable or constitutes an abuse of process. Initiating parallel proceedings to achieve an outcome (such as altering a board of directors) that is explicitly prohibited by an active interim injunction in another suit is an impermissible collateral attack and a clear abuse of process.
Party_Details.Application: The legal conflict concerns the procedural validity of a shareholder seeking to imply terms into the company’s constitution while a separate injunction in a related suit governs the board's composition.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court agreed completely with N Century's arguments and struck out the originating application. The judge ruled that Canstone's application was a blatant abuse of process because it operated as a direct collateral attack on the interim injunction protecting the board's composition in HC/OC 798/2023. If Canstone wished to alter the board, the legally correct procedure was to seek leave to intervene in the suit where the injunction was granted, rather than filing a separate, conflicting action. Furthermore, the court adopted N Century's argument that Canstone's request to imply terms into the constitution was factually and legally unsustainable; there was no necessary gap in the constitution, as existing statutory and constitutional rules allowed for director removal via ordinary resolution. Because the claims were abusive and legally defective, the court struck out the action in its entirety against all defendants to prevent the risk of inconsistent judgments or adverse effects on N Century's protected position, awarding costs in N Century's favor.
